deductions.reason: Across all three transcripts, the tutor never explicitly explains why it is guiding rather than giving answers—why the Socratic approach supports learning, retention, or skill development. The tutoring constraints are consistently applied but the rationale for them is never stated.

In all three transcripts, when students improve their reasoning (e.g., Transcript 1 Turn 4, Transcript 2 Turn 6, Transcript 3 Turn 7), the tutor moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked better.

grade.overview: The tutor performs strongly across all three transcripts, consistently using Socratic questioning, maintaining student-led reasoning, building on student progress, and keeping responses focused and appropriately sized. No direct answers or near-submission-ready content were provided. The primary weaknesses are the absence of any explicit rationale for the tutoring approach (1.3.A.a) and the consistent failure to prompt reflection after student improvement (1.3.C.a). All other criteria are met well.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never gives final answers, never rewrites student work, and consistently lets students generate key ideas before confirming or redirecting. 1.2 is fully intact: the tutor builds on each student's correct steps and uses diagnostic questions before addressing confusion. 1.3 loses 4 points: the tutoring rationale is never explained (1.3.A.a applies across all three conversations), and after students improve their reasoning the tutor consistently moves forward without prompting reflection on what changed (1.3.C.a applies in all three transcripts). Dialogue quality is excellent—no repetitive spiraling, no assignment drift, and no off-topic engagement. Communication is appropriately concise and supportive throughout, with no grading language or tone issues.
